title: Hearing - Sales and/or Use Tax (State,Local,MTA) - 202511017H
text:
SOAH DOCKET NO. 304-26-00693 

CPA HEARING NO. 119,043

 

RE:      **************



TAXPAYER NO: **************

AUDIT OFFICE: **************

AUDIT PERIOD: September 1, 2017 THROUGH November 30, 2020 

 

Sales And Use Tax/RDT

 

BEFORE THE COMPTROLLER OF PUBLIC ACCOUNTS

OF THE STATE OF TEXAS

 

KELLY HANCOCK

Acting Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts

 

MOLLY BUCHANAN

Representing Respondent

 

**************

Representing Petitioner


 


 

COMPTROLLER’S DECISION

 

This decision is considered final on December 19, 2025 unless a motion for rehearing is timely filed; this date of finality is calculated based on the Administrative Procedure Act (APA). [ENDNOTE 1] The failure to timely file a motion for rehearing may result in adverse legal consequences.

 

Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Trevor Moore of the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) issued a Proposal for Decision (PFD) that includes Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law. SOAH served the PFD on each party and each party was given an opportunity to file exceptions and replies with SOAH in accordance with SOAH’s rules of procedure. The ALJ recommended that the Comptroller adopt the PFD as written.

 

After review and consideration, IT IS ORDERED that the PFD is adopted as written.

 

The result from this Decision is Attachment A. The ALJ’s recommendation letter is Attachment B. The PFD as written is Attachment C. Attachments A, B, and C are incorporated by reference.

 

Attachment A reflects a liability. [ENDNOTE 2]

 


The total sum of the tax, penalty, and interest is due and payable 20 days after a comptroller’s decision becomes final.3 If such sum is not timely paid, an additional penalty of 10 percent of the taxes due will accrue.

 

SIGNED on this 24th day of November 2025

 

KELLY HANCOCK

Acting Comptroller of Public Accounts

 

By:  Lisa Craven

Deputy Comptroller

 

Attachment A, Texas Notification of Hearing Results 

Attachment B, ALJ’s recommendation letter 

Attachment C, Proposal for Decision as written

 

 

 

ATTACHMENT C

 

 

SOAH Docket No. 304-26-00693                                              

Before the State Office of Administrative Hearings

TCPA Hearing No. 119,043

 

 

Proposal for Decision

 

The Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) audited ************** (Petitioner) for sales and use tax compliance and issued an assessment that included tax, 10% penalty, and accrued interest. Petitioner requested a redetermination of the assessment, raising a general claim of error in each audit exam and stating that it would provide Staff with additional documentation for review. Petitioner did not submit supporting documentation to Staff and did not submit argument or evidence for the written submission hearing. In this Proposal for Decision, the Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) finds the audit assessment should be upheld.

 

I. Notice, Jurisdiction, and Procedural History

 

Staff referred the contested case to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) and, on September 11, 2025, issued a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. On September 16, 2025, ALJ Trevor Moore issued the Order Setting Written Submission Hearing. ************** of the COMPANY. represented Petitioner, and Molly Anne Buchanan represented Staff. The record closed on October 27, 2025.

 

There are no additional issues of notice or jurisdiction; therefore, those matters are set out in the Findings of Fact and Conclusions of Law without further discussion.

 

II. Reasons for Decision

 

A. Evidence

 

Petitioner did not submit evidence for the written submission hearing. Staff offered the following evidentiary exhibits:


	
	Hearing Request Receipt;
	
	
	Hearing Grant Letter;
	
	
	Intro Letter;
	
	
	Texas Notification of Audit Results;
	
	
	Penalty and Interest Waiver Worksheet;
	
	
	Audit Report; and
	
	
	Audit Plan.
	


 

Staff’s exhibits were admitted without objection.

 

B. Agreements

 

Staff agreed to waive interest accrued in the period of August 1, 2023, through July 31, 2024.

 

C. Material Facts

 

During the period at issue, Petitioner operated a business providing power washing services in and around CITY, Texas.

 

In December 2020, Staff initiated a sales and use tax compliance audit of Petitioner for the period of September 1, 2017, through November 30, 2020.

 

At the outset of the audit, the auditor requested that Petitioner provide its business records for the audit period. Petitioner provided sales journals, federal income tax returns, financial statements, general ledgers, accounts payable journals, and purchase invoices for the audit. Relying on Petitioner’s business records, the auditor performed a reconciliation of the tax amounts shown in Petitioner’s sales records to the tax reported for the corresponding periods. The unreported tax amounts were scheduled as tax collected but not remitted in Exam 100. Unsupported deductions for nontaxed sales were scheduled as additional taxable sales in Exam 200, and additional taxable sales reflected in Petitioner’s general ledger were scheduled in Exam 300.

 

On October 22, 2021, Staff issued the Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner assessing sales and use tax, 10% penalty, and accrued interest. Petitioner requested a redetermination of the assessment, generally contending each audit exam was in error and stating that additional records would be provided to Staff in support of its contentions. Petitioner also requested waiver of the penalty and interest assessments. Petitioner did not submit additional records for consideration during the redetermination at the Comptroller, and the case was referred to SOAH for a written submission hearing. Petitioner did not submit evidence or argument for the written submission hearing.

 

D. ALJ ANALYSIS

 

Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051. The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Id. § 151.010. All sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. Id.; see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 116,305 (2021), 30,461 (1994). However, only services specifically enumerated in Section 151.0101 are taxable. Therefore, to impose tax on a service, Staff must demonstrate not only that a service was sold but that the service is taxable. Id. If Staff demonstrates, prima facie, that a service is taxable and that an exclusion does not apply, it becomes the taxpayer’s burden to prove, by a preponderance of the evidence, that the service is not taxable, or by clear and convincing evidence that the taxable service was exempt from taxation. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26.

 

Taxable real property services include the removal or collection of non-hazardous garbage, rubbish or solid waste as well as residential or nonresidential building or grounds cleaning, janitorial, or custodial services such as keeping the inside and outside premises of a building clean, orderly, and functional, including performing minor adjustments, maintenance, or repairs. Tex. Tax Code § 151.0048(a)(3),(4); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.356(a)(7). Power washing of improvements to real property has consistently been considered to be building or grounds cleaning, a taxable real property service. See State Tax Automated Research (STAR) Document Nos. 9202L1155B07 (February 13, 1992), 9205L1172D01 (May 18, 1992), and 200006386L (June 7, 2000).

 

The repair, remodeling, maintenance, and restoration of tangible personal property is a taxable service. Tex. Tax Code § 151.0101(a)(5). When performed on tangible personal property, power washing services are taxable as the restoration or maintenance of tangible personal property. See Comptroller’s Publication No. 94-111 (2006).

 

The ALJ concludes that Staff’s evidence is sufficient to establish, prima facie, that Petitioner made taxable sales of real property services as well as services for the repair, remodeling, maintenance, and restoration of tangible personal property during the audit period. The evidence supports a conclusion that the audit methodology was authorized by statute and the best information available was utilized and it is Petitioner’s burden to prove the transactions that remain at issue were nontaxable or tax exempt. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(c), (e).

 

In a contested case hearing a taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. Tex. Tax Code § 111.0041; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a). A taxpayer is generally required to support its contentions with source records, and assertions in pleadings are insufficient to meet the burden of proof. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 105,892 (2012).

 

Petitioner did not identify the specific transactions that remain at issue or submit argument or evidence for the written submission hearing. Petitioner’s claims of audit error are unsubstantiated and must be denied.

 

Petitioner requests waiver of penalty. Late penalties are automatically imposed on delinquent taxes. Tex. Tax Code § 111.061. The Comptroller has the discretionary authority to waive late penalties if a taxpayer has exercised reasonable diligence to comply with tax laws. Id. § 111.103. A taxpayer must establish reasonable diligence by a preponderance of evidence. See Comptroller’s Decision No. 102,695 (2010).

 

In making the reasonable diligence determination, the Comptroller considers the following factors: the taxpayer’s audit history, the tax issues involved, whether a change in Comptroller policy occurred during the audit period, whether changes in the law took effect during the audit period, the size and sophistication of the taxpayer, whether tax was collected but not remitted, whether returns were timely filed, the completeness of the taxpayer’s records, the taxpayer’s efforts to comply with the recordkeeping requirements of this state, delinquencies in other taxes, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, and the error rate in the current audit. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(b)(3), (d). To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that penalty waiver is warranted. See id. § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 102,268 (2014).

 

In this case, the assessment had an overall error rate of over 92%, there is a substantial amount of tax collected that was not remitted to the state, and Petitioner had 30 delinquent returns during the audit period. This is not consistent with reasonable diligence to comply with the tax laws, and the presence of tax collected but not remitted is contraindicative of the exercise of reasonable diligence. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 118,392 (2024). The ALJ finds the 10% penalty should be upheld.

 

Petitioner requested a waiver of interest. Delinquent taxes draw interest beginning 60 days after the date due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.060(c). The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive interest assessments and may exercise his discretion if interest was imposed as a result of undue delay caused by Comptroller personnel, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, or natural disaster. Id. § 111.103; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(e). To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that interest waiver is warranted. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,268 and 109,069 (2014).

 

Staff agreed to waive interest for the period of April 1, 2024, to August 31, 2024. The ALJ finds Petitioner did not propose any period of undue delay and therefore did not establish, by a preponderance of the evidence, that additional interest waiver is warranted.

 

III. Findings of Fact


	
	During the period at issue, ************** (Petitioner) operated a business providing power washing services in and around CITY, Texas.
	
	
	In December 2020, the Tax Division (Staff) of the Texas Comptroller of Public Accounts (Comptroller) initiated a sales and use tax compliance audit of Petitioner for the period of September 1, 2017, through November 30, 2020.
	
	
	At the outset of the audit the auditor requested that Petitioner provide its business records for the audit period. Petitioner provided sales journals, federal income tax returns, financial statements, general ledgers, accounts payable journals, and purchase invoices for the audit.
	
	
	Relying on Petitioner’s business records, the auditor performed a reconciliation of the tax amounts shown in Petitioner’s sales records to the tax reported for the corresponding periods. The unreported tax amounts were scheduled as tax collected but not remitted in Exam 100. Unsupported deductions for nontaxed sales were scheduled as additional taxable sales in Exam 200, and additional taxable sales reflected in Petitioner’s general ledger were scheduled in Exam 300.
	
	
	On October 22, 2021, Staff issued the Texas Notification of Audit Results to Petitioner assessing sales and use tax, 10% penalty, and accrued interest.
	
	
	The assessment had an overall error rate of over 92%, and Petitioner had 30 delinquent returns during the audit period.
	
	
	Petitioner requested a redetermination of the audit.
	
	
	Staff referred the matter to the State Office of Administrative Hearings (SOAH) for a hearing on the merits.
	
	
	On September 11, 2025, Staff issued a Notice of Hearing by Written Submission. The notice contained a statement of the nature of the hearing; a statement of the legal authority and jurisdiction under which the hearing was to be held; a reference to the particular sections of the statutes and rules involved; and a short, plain statement of the factual matters asserted, or an attachment that incorporated by reference the factual matters asserted in the complaint or petition filed with the state agency.
	
	
	On September 16, 2025, Administrative Law Judge (ALJ) Trevor Moore issued the Order Setting Written Submission Hearing.
	
	
	The contested case record closed on October 27, 2025.
	
	
	Petitioner did not submit argument or evidence for the written submission hearing.
	


 

IV. Conclusions of Law


	
	The Comptroller has jurisdiction over this matter. See Tex. Tax Code ch. 111.
	
	
	SOAH has jurisdiction over matters related to the hearing in this matter, including the authority to issue a proposal for decision with findings of fact and conclusions of law. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2003.
	
	
	Staff provided proper and timely notice of the hearing. See Tex. Gov’t Code ch. 2001; Tex. Tax Code § 111.009.
	
	
	Texas imposes a tax on each sale of a taxable item in this state. Tex. Tax Code § 151.051.
	
	
	The term “taxable item” includes tangible personal property and taxable services. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010.
	
	
	All sales of tangible personal property in this state are taxable. Tex. Tax Code § 151.010; see also, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 116,305 (2021), 30,461 (1994).
	
	
	Only services specifically enumerated in Texas Tax Code § 151.0101 are taxable.
	
	
	If Staff demonstrates, prima facie, that a service is taxable and that an exclusion does not apply, it becomes the taxpayer’s burden to prove, by a preponderance of the evidence, that the service is not taxable, or by clear and convincing evidence that the taxable service was exempt from taxation. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26.
	
	
	Taxable real property services include the removal or collection of non-hazardous garbage, rubbish or solid waste as well as residential or nonresidential building or grounds cleaning, janitorial, or custodial services such as keeping the inside and outside premises of a building clean, orderly, and functional, including performing minor adjustments, maintenance, or repairs. Tex. Tax Code § 151.0048(a)(3),(4); 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.356(a)(7).
	
	
	Power washing of improvements to real property has consistently been considered to be building or grounds cleaning, a taxable real property service. See State Tax Automated Research (STAR) Document Nos. 9202L1155B07 (February 13, 1992), 9205L1172D01 (May 18, 1992), and 200006386L (June 7, 2000).
	
	
	The repair, remodeling, maintenance, and restoration of tangible personal property is a taxable service. Tex. Tax Code § 151.0101(a)(5).
	
	
	When performed on tangible personal property, power washing services are taxable as the restoration or maintenance of tangible personal property. See Comptroller’s Publication No. 94-111 (2006).
	
	
	Staff’s evidence is sufficient to establish, prima facie, that Petitioner made taxable sales of real property services as well as services for the repair, remodeling, maintenance, and restoration of tangible personal property during the audit period.
	
	
	The audit methodology was authorized by statute and the best information available was utilized.
	
	
	It is Petitioner’s burden to prove the transactions that remain at issue were nontaxable or tax exempt. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(c), (e).
	
	
	In a contested case hearing a taxpayer must produce sufficient records and supporting documentation appropriate to the tax or fee for the transactions in question to substantiate and enable verification of the taxpayer’s claim related to the amount of tax, penalty, or interest to be assessed, collected, or refunded. Tex. Tax Code § 111.0041; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(a).
	
	
	Petitioner failed to demonstrate error in the audit assessment.
	
	
	Late penalties are automatically imposed on delinquent taxes. Tex. Tax Code § 111.061.
	
	
	The Comptroller has the discretionary authority to waive late penalties if a taxpayer has exercised reasonable diligence to comply with tax laws. Tex. Tax Code § 111.103.
	
	
	In making the reasonable diligence determination, the Comptroller considers the following factors: the taxpayer’s audit history, the tax issues involved, whether a change in Comptroller policy occurred during the audit period, whether changes in the law took effect during the audit period, the size and sophistication of the taxpayer, whether tax was collected but not remitted, whether returns were timely filed, the completeness of the taxpayer’s records, the taxpayer’s efforts to comply with the recordkeeping requirements of this state, delinquencies in other taxes, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, and the error rate in the current audit. 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(b)(3), (d).
	
	
	The presence of tax collected but not remitted is contraindicative of the exercise of reasonable diligence. See, e.g., Comptroller’s Decision No. 118,392 (2024).
	
	
	Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that penalty waiver is warranted. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision No. 102,268 (2014).
	
	
	The ALJ finds Petitioner did not demonstrate reasonable diligence to comply with the tax law.
	
	
	The 10% penalty should be upheld.
	
	
	Delinquent taxes draw interest beginning 60 days after the date due. Tex. Tax Code § 111.060(c).
	
	
	The Comptroller has discretionary authority to waive interest assessments and may exercise his discretion if interest was imposed as a result of undue delay caused by Comptroller personnel, reliance on advice provided by the Comptroller’s office, or natural disaster. Tex. Tax Code § 111.060(c). § 111.103; 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 3.5(e).
	
	
	To prevail, Petitioner must demonstrate, by a preponderance of the evidence, that interest waiver is warranted. See 34 Tex. Admin. Code § 1.26(e); see also Comptroller’s Decision Nos. 102,268 and 109,069 (2014).
	
	
	The ALJ finds Petitioner did not establish, by a preponderance of the evidence, that additional interest waiver is warranted.
	
	
	The assessment should be upheld, except for the interest waiver agreed by Staff.
	


 

SIGNED October 30, 2025.

 

Trevor Moore

Presiding Administrative Law Judge

 

 

 

ENDNOTES


[1] The date calculated is 25 days after this decision is signed. See APA, Tex. Gov’t Code § 2001.146(a); S.B. 1095, Acts 2017, 85th Leg. For additional guidance, refer to the Frequently Asked Questions Related to Motions for Rehearing, found here: http://comptroller.texas.gov/taxes/publications/96-1789.pdf

[2] At present, insufficient information is available to determine which items and amounts are disputed or undisputed for purposes of Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112. In the absence of this information, the Comptroller will assume the entire amount of the assessment, as it appears in Comptroller’s Decision Attachment A, the Notification of Hearing Results, remains in dispute.

If Petitioner intends to sue the comptroller to dispute an amount of tax, penalty, or interest assessed in a deficiency redetermination or jeopardy determination under Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 111, Petitioner is required to file a motion for rehearing that “states the specific grounds of error and the disputed amounts associated with the grounds of error.” Tex. Tax Code § 112.201(a)(3). Petitioner should refer to Tex. Tax Code, Ch. 112, for further guidance regarding a suit after redetermination.

[3] See Tex. Tax Code § 111.0081(c).
subjects: PENALTY WAIVER-TAX COLLECTED FROM CUSTOMERS AND NOT REMITTED
PENALTY WAIVER-LATE/DELINQUENT RETURNS-FAILURE TO TIMELY FILE RETURNS
PENALTY WAIVER-ERROR PERCENTAGE VERY HIGH/ERROR RATE INCREASED FOR AUDIT PERIOD-DENIED
